led on Monday, extending a two-week slump, after the company took a first s=
tep toward allowing the former president and other insiders to capitalize o=
n their stakes.Trump Media & Technology Group Corp., parent of Truth Social=
, filed to register shares, including those linked to warrants. The move co=
uld ultimately bring forward sales from insiders that are currently not per=
mitted until September.Here is that registration statement, which is not a =
surprise =E2=80=94=C2=A0Trump Media was obligated to register these shares =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0but which is, I suppose, new. The very rough numbers are
  [1]
:=09Trump Media has about 136.7 million shares outstanding.=09Of those, abo=
ut 28.7 million shares are held by public shareholders and trade freely.=09=
About 78.8 million shares are held by Donald Trump, which he can=E2=80=99t =
sell until late September.=09The remaining 30 million-ish shares=C2=A0are m=
ostly held by other sorts of insiders: Trump=E2=80=99s partners in starting=
 the company, early investors, the promoters of the special purpose acquisi=
tion company that took it public. Many of these are also subject to=C2=A0lo=
ckups that end=C2=A0in September.=09People =E2=80=94=C2=A0early investors, =
hedge funds =E2=80=94=C2=A0also own warrants=C2=A0to buy about another 21.5=
 million shares at $11.50 per share. Those warrants should be exercisable s=
oon =E2=80=94=C2=A0when that registration statement gets through US Securit=
ies and Exchange Commission review =E2=80=94=C2=A0and, if the stock is stil=
l above $11.50 (it closed at $26.61 yesterday),=C2=A0you=E2=80=99d expect t=
he holders to exercise the warrants and sell the shares.
  [2]
=09If the stock stays above $17.50 for the next week or so, the company wil=
l issue 40 million new shares to insiders, 36 million of them to Trump hims=
elf.So there are on the order of 200 million total shares (shares currently=
 outstanding plus shares soon to be issued), of which perhaps=C2=A030 milli=
on are currently trading.
  [3]
 In the next few weeks, another 20 million might come on the market. By Sep=
tember, another 150 million shares could=C2=A0theoretically=C2=A0come on th=
e market, though it=E2=80=99s not like all the insiders will dump their sha=
res as soon as they can. Right?Your simple model could be that there is a s=
table-ish price for Trump Media as a meme stock with 30 million shares to t=
rade, but the price for a meme stock with 50 million shares to trade, or 20=
0 million, would be proportionally lower.=C2=A0Or I guess a model for a mem=
e stock could go something like this. It starts with a burst of attention: =
In Trump Media=E2=80=99s case, the stock soared in October 2021, when the s=
pecial purpose acquisition company first announced (the existence of and) i=
ts merger with Trump Media, and then again in January 2024, when the merger=
 looked likely to close, and in March, when it did.=C2=A0And these bursts o=
f attention took Trump Media=E2=80=99s stock price way, way, way, way, way =
beyond what seemed to be justified by its fundamentals (as a money-losing c=
ompany with $4 million of revenue). That is sort of the definition of a mem=
e stock.=C2=A0Then what? Well:=09Probably=C2=A0that attention =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0and thus the stock price =E2=80=94=C2=A0will decay over time, if nothing=
 else happens. If Trump Media=E2=80=99s stock is literally and purely a sto=
ck-market token of fondness for Donald Trump, and never announces new produ=
cts or makes any money, then=C2=A0probably=C2=A0its retail investors will l=
ose interest and the stock will drift down. This is not necessarily=C2=A0tr=
ue, and you could imagine =E2=80=94=C2=A0I have imagined =E2=80=94=C2=A0the=
 token taking on an economic life of its own with no long-term connection t=
o the fundamentals of the business. But my best guess is that a stock can=
=E2=80=99t trade forever as a pure meme.=09The bull case is that the compan=
y grows into the stock price: Use all that attention, and the money that it=
 can raise, to build products and justify its valuation. You know, make som=
e product announcements, have earnings, that sort of thing.=09The bear case=
 is =E2=80=A6=C2=A0look, it has never been particularly difficult to identi=
fy the likely bagholders in the Trump Media trade? Or the timing? This is a=
 stock with (1) a bunch of retail investors buying, (2) a bunch of hedge fu=
nds who have warrants that they can probably exercise this month or so and =
(3) a bunch of insiders (really one main insider) who need money and will b=
e able to sell their stock in September or so. If the attention persists un=
til they can sell =E2=80=94=C2=A0or if there are product announcements or p=
ositive earnings along the way that keep the stock up =E2=80=94=C2=A0then, =
I mean, what do I know, but wouldn=E2=80=99t you expect them to extract as =
much of the meme-stock value as possible for themselves?When Trump Media we=
nt public as a meme stock, it=C2=A0set off a race between its business and =
its big shareholders=E2=80=99 ability to sell. This week brought news on bo=
th sides =E2=80=94=C2=A0the streaming plans for the business, the warrant s=
hare registration for the sales =E2=80=94=C2=A0and, uh. The stock is down.M=
eanwhile here is a quite grim Washington Post story interviewing some of th=
e shareholders:Jerry Dean McLain first bet on former president Donald Trump=
's Truth Social two years ago, buying into the Trump company's planned merg=
er partner, Digital World Acquisition, at $90 a share. Over time, as the pr=
ice changed, he kept buying, amassing hundreds of shares for $25,000 =E2=80=
=94 pretty much his "whole nest egg," he said.That nest egg has lost about =
half its value in the past two weeks as Trump Media & Technology Group's sh=
are price dropped from $66 after its public debut last month to $32 on Frid=
ay. But McLain, 71, who owns a tree-removal service outside Oklahoma City, =
said he's not worried. If anything, he wants to buy more."I know good and w=
ell it's in Trump's hands, and he's got plans," he said. "I have no doubt i=
t's going to explode sometime."=C2=A0As with many meme stocks, there are co=
nspiracy theories about what could possibly be keeping Trump Media trading =
at only 800 times trailing revenues:Todd Schlanger, an interior designer at=
 a furniture store in West Palm Beach who said Trump had been one of his cu=
stomers=C2=A0=E2=80=A6 suspects the recent drops in share price have been t=
he result of "stock manipulation" from an "organized effort" to make the co=
mpany look bad. There's no proof of such a campaign, but Schlanger is convi=
nced. "It's got to be political," he said, from all the "liberals that are =
trying to knock it down." ...Carol Swain, a prominent conservative commenta=
tor in Nashville who previously taught political science at Vanderbilt Univ=
ersity =E2=80=A6=C2=A0suspects stock manipulation, arguing that "the people=
 who hate Donald Trump would do anything to try to hurt him." As for Truth =
Social itself, she said she posts there only sparingly and prefers X, where=
 she has 35 times as many followers. "I have always wanted not to just prea=
ch to the choir," she said.McLain, the tree service owner in Oklahoma, said=
 he believes the stock could "go to $1,000 a share, easy," once the media s=
tops writing so negatively about it and the company works through its growi=
ng pains. The company's leaders, he said, are being "too silent right now" =
amid questions about the falling share price, but he suspects it's because =
they're working on something amazing and new.I hope he=E2=80=99s happy with=
 the streaming announcement.

      Hunterbrook
   =20
 =20
We have talked a few times about Hunterbrook, the hedge fund that is also a=
 newspaper:=09Hunterbrook Media is a media organization that publishes gene=
ral news and investigative journalism.=09Before publishing an investigative=
 story, it runs it by Hunterbrook Capital, its affiliated hedge fund.=09If =
the investigation looks like it will move the market, Hunterbrook Capital w=
ill do a trade to profit from the investigation.=09These profits pay for Hu=
nterbrook Media=E2=80=99s newsroom.Earlier this month,=C2=A0Hunterbrook lau=
nched its website with a big investigative report on United Wholesale Mortg=
age, which Hunterbrook Media accused of misconduct;=C2=A0Hunterbrook Capita=
l shorted the stock of its parent company,=C2=A0UWM Holdings Corp. (The sto=
ck closed yesterday=C2=A0down about 7.5% from where it was before the repor=
t.)Hunterbrook=C2=A0was back yesterday with a big investigative report on P=
osco, the Korean conglomerate, which Hunterbrook Media accuses of complicit=
y with the=C2=A0junta in Myanmar
  [4]
:A major source of the money that the junta uses for jet fuel and weapons i=
s Myanmar=E2=80=99s four offshore gas projects. Of those, the Shwe project,=
 operated by Posco International Corp. in joint ownership with an entity co=
ntrolled by the junta, has been the most profitable in recent years, accord=
ing to a report by the U.N. Special Rapporteur on the situation of human ri=
ghts in Myanmar. The report claims Shwe provided over a half billion dollar=
s per year to the regime in the two years after the coup, helping fund a $1=
 billion spending spree for arms, including fighter jets, attack drones, as=
sault helicopters, tank parts, and advanced missile systems.The purchases h=
ave enabled what the U.N. Special Rapporteur called =E2=80=9Cprobable crime=
s against humanity,=E2=80=9D including murder, torture, sexual violence, an=
d the pillaging of villages.=C2=A0After the coup, major energy companies fr=
om Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development member countries =
that were operating Myanmar=E2=80=99s offshore gas projects left, citing hu=
man rights violations. Not Posco.Posco not only chose to stay, but continue=
d to pour in hundreds of millions of dollars to increase production at the =
Shwe gas project, even as evidence of junta atrocities grew. =C2=A0But righ=
t at the top there is a statement that =E2=80=9CHunterbrook Media=E2=80=99s=
 investment affiliate, Hunterbrook Capital, did not take any positions rela=
ted to this article.=E2=80=9DWhy not? Why would Hunterbrook invest resource=
s in doing a big international=C2=A0investigation, find alleged wrongdoing =
at a publicly traded company, and then not trade? Let me propose a few theo=
retically possible=C2=A0answers:=09Sometimes, in investigating a company, H=
unterbrook Media will find stuff that (1) is bad for the world and (2) the =
public ought to know about, but (3) is not bad=C2=A0for business. =E2=80=9C=
A big international trading company is complicit in human rights abuses=E2=
=80=9D might just be that kind of story? Doing business with bad people mig=
ht be morally bad,=C2=A0but good for the bottom line. As a journalistic end=
eavor, Hunterbrook Media might want to call attention to these abuses. As a=
 profit-seeking hedge fund, Hunterbrook Capital might pass. (Posco Internat=
ional=E2=80=99s stock is down this week.)=09Sometimes, in investigating a c=
ompany, Hunterbrook Media=E2=80=99s journalists might learn of wrongdoing f=
rom inside sources at the company, and might rely on leaked internal docume=
nts to support it. Probably not =E2=80=94=C2=A0Hunterbrook says =E2=80=9Cwe=
 avoid talking to insiders, depending instead on publicly available informa=
tion=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0but perhaps sometimes the journalists are temp=
ted.=C2=A0These are, after all, traditional tools of investigative journali=
sm. But =E2=80=94=C2=A0as we discussed the first time Hunterbrook appeared =
in this column =E2=80=94=C2=A0they are risky tools for=C2=A0hedge funds. Tr=
ading based on inside sources and internal documents is arguably illegal in=
sider trading. You could imagine=C2=A0Hunterbrook Media doing some traditio=
nal investigative journalism, and then=C2=A0Hunterbrook Capital=C2=A0saying=
 =E2=80=9Cnope, this has inside information, we have to=C2=A0pass.=E2=80=9D=
=09Sometimes Hunterbrook Media will do an investigation that (1) reveals a =
good trade and (2) gets through compliance, but perhaps Hunterbrook Capital=
 will pass on the trade in order to build up the credibility of Hunterbrook=
 Media. =E2=80=9CSee, it=E2=80=99s real investigative journalism; it=E2=80=
=99s not=C2=A0just=C2=A0finding trades for a hedge fund.=E2=80=9D They are =
playing a long game here, and I suppose the goal is to become a trusted inv=
estigative news outlet rather than just another activist short fund publish=
ing occasional splashy short reports.=09Sometimes, in investigating a compa=
ny, Hunterbrook Media will find out bad stuff about a company in South Kore=
a. And it will take its results to Hunterbrook Capital, which will say =E2=
=80=9Cah yes, this is great, this will crush=C2=A0the stock.=E2=80=9D And t=
hey=E2=80=99ll run it by compliance, which will say =E2=80=9Cah yes, this i=
s all based on public information, you=E2=80=99re free to trade on it.=E2=
=80=9D And Hunterbrook Capital will decide to put on a big short bet agains=
t=C2=A0the company. And then it will remember that South Korea=E2=80=99s st=
ock market=C2=A0currently bans short sales, whoops!
 =20
   =20
      Whistleblowers and short sellers
   =20
 =20
One thesis you could have is:=09Activist short selling of fraudulent public=
 companies is a social good: It is good that there are people out there who=
 are investigating public companies, finding the ones that are doing bad st=
uff, shorting their stocks and publicizing the bad stuff. It is good that t=
here is a market incentive to discover and disclose financial fraud; it mak=
es markets fairer and more efficient and more honest.=09The incentives are,=
 however, insufficient. Activist short selling is=C2=A0hard. Most stocks go=
 up. The bad ones are often expensive to short due to high stock-borrow cos=
ts. If you get your activist short report wrong, you will get sued. The com=
panies that you short will get mad and seek vengeance. Short sellers are wi=
dely misunderstood and disliked, and there=E2=80=99s a risk of criminal inv=
estigation=C2=A0or getting run over by a meme stock.=09For all those reason=
s, there is less=C2=A0activist short selling than would be socially optimal=
.=09The government should correct this market failure by subsidizing activi=
st short selling. If you are an activist short seller, and you short a comp=
any because you think it=E2=80=99s a fraud, and you turn out to be right, t=
he government should write you a check =E2=80=94=C2=A0in=C2=A0addition=C2=
=A0to your winnings on your trade =E2=80=94=C2=A0to encourage you.When I wr=
ite it like that it seems =E2=80=A6=C2=A0wrong? As a general matter, it=E2=
=80=99s not obvious that the best use of government resources is to subsidi=
ze=C2=A0any=C2=A0sort of hedge funds. Also, some of the problems with short=
 selling are imposed by the government =E2=80=94=C2=A0the hazy criminal inv=
estigations, for instance
  [5]
=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0and it might be more efficient to get rid of those pro=
blems than to offset them with subsidies.Nonetheless this is a real thing t=
hat the government actually does! Sort of. Here=E2=80=99s a fun paper by Al=
exander Platt of the University of Kansas School of Law titled =E2=80=9CThe=
 Shortseller Enrichment Commission?: Whistleblowers, Activist Short Sellers=
, and the New Privatization of Public Enforcement=E2=80=9D:Two developments=
 have transformed the detection of corporate fraud in the last decade: the =
SEC whistleblower bounty program (WBP) and the rise of activist short selle=
rs. The WBP offers up financial bounties to individuals who bring forward a=
ctionable information about securities fraud. Activist shorts conduct due d=
iligence to identify overvalued public companies, take short positions, rev=
eal the negative information, and then enjoy trading profits if and when th=
e stock tanks. Considered separately, these institutions are widely regarde=
d as socially valuable innovations that help deter fraud.But, it turns out,=
 they are not fully separate. Activist shorts have been participating activ=
ely and effectively in the WBP =E2=80=93 both directly (submitting tips, fi=
ling claims, and winning awards) and indirectly (partnering with insider ti=
psters). Their participation has transformed the WBP into an undercover out=
sourcing program; a new way for the SEC to pay private professionals to do =
work that traditionally has been done by SEC staff.This privatization might=
 be defensible, indeed laudable, if it yielded more efficient deterrence th=
an what the SEC could achieve on its own. Unfortunately, it likely does not=
. The SEC may be paying activist shorts for information they would have mad=
e public even without the prospect of bounty (because of their trading stra=
tegy). In such cases, the public bounty payment does nothing to incentivize=
 additional fraud detection and is merely a windfall for the recipient. Tho=
se funds could be better spent by expanding the SEC=E2=80=99s own enforceme=
nt capacity.We have talked before about the increasing=C2=A0synergies betwe=
en activist short selling and whistleblowing.=C2=A0Notably, when we discuss=
ed Hunterbrook=E2=80=99s first published investigation of a public company,=
 I noted that it shorted the stock, got long a competitor=E2=80=99s stock, =
filed some lawsuits and also filed an SEC whistleblower complaint. =E2=80=
=9CIf you can find out bad news about companies, there are a lot of ways to=
 make money,=E2=80=9D I wrote. Possibly too many.
 =20
   =20
      Silence is not securities fraud
   =20
 =20
Under US securities law, if a company=C2=A0says something false, it can be =
sued for securities fraud, but if it=C2=A0neglects to mention something tru=
e and important,=C2=A0it can=E2=80=99t. There are lots of qualifications to=
 that second part, to the point that I kind of discount it, but we talked y=
esterday about a Supreme Court decision holding that =E2=80=9Cpure omission=
s=E2=80=9D are not securities fraud. I wrote:You could imagine pure silence=
 being misleading. For instance, you could imagine things that the market w=
ould=C2=A0expect to be disclosed=C2=A0if they happened, so that=C2=A0not=C2=
=A0disclosing them sort of implies that they didn=E2=80=99t happen. If a co=
mpany=E2=80=99s chief executive officer died suddenly, and the company didn=
=E2=80=99t tell anyone for a few weeks, that would be weird! The company ha=
sn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0lied, but everyone kind of went around assuming that the =
CEO was alive, and the company didn=E2=80=99t bother to correct their mista=
ken impression.Readers emailed to suggest considerable improvements over th=
at hypothetical example. Seil Kim pointed me to a 2023 paper that he wrote =
with Seungjoon Oh with this amazing footnote:As an extreme example, one of =
the sample firms in this study, Badger Paper Mills, delayed its announcemen=
t of the death of its CEO for about two months until the next scheduled reg=
ular board meeting. According to one of the directors, =E2=80=9Cthe board d=
ecided to wait to disclose the search for Cosgrove=E2=80=99s replacement un=
til it also had news of a new board member=E2=80=9D (Milwaukee Journal Sent=
inel, October 30, 2000). Interestingly, the SEC explicitly states that the =
death of an officer or director does not trigger an 8-K filing (https://lin=
k.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35055872.287342/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdv=
di9kaXZpc2lvbnMvY29ycGZpbi9ndWlkYW5jZS84LWtpbnRlcnAuaHRtKS5BbmQ/607f07ceef4=
b8524a319a23cB41836191 Evan Hughes=C2=A0pointed out a slightly different re=
al case=C2=A0from Sweden=C2=A0in 2016:Swedish measurement technology firm H=
exagon has defended the time it took to announce the arrest of its chief ex=
ecutive for alleged insider trading after it came to light he was under arr=
est during last week's earnings call with analysts.After being detained in =
Sweden on Oct. 26, Ola Rollen was allowed by the Swedish Economic Crime Aut=
hority to present Hexagon's third-quarter results in a conference call on O=
ct. 28, the agency told Reuters on Tuesday, adding two of its police office=
rs were with Rollen during the call.Analysts on the call were not told Roll=
en had been arrested or that police were in the room.=C2=A0Yes, right, weir=
d thing not to mention.

  [1] See page 17 of the merger proxy for the rough breakdown, Donald Trump=
=E2=80=99s Schedule 13D for more recent numbers and the lockup terms, and t=
he registration statement for the warrant numbers.


  [2] It is not generally optimal for holders to exercise an option early, =
but (1) this situation is weird and (2) as is typical with SPAC warrants, T=
rump Media can (and probably should) effectively force them to exercise if =
the stock is above $18. (See page 55 of the registration statement.) If the=
 stock is below $18 but above $11.50, it=E2=80=99s less obvious that the wa=
rrants would be exercised, though apparently a lot of warrant holders are h=
edged with the expensive-to-borrow stock and so might exercise early.


  [3] I see about 5.9 million shares of short interest, so maybe more like =
36 million shares effectively available to trade.


  [4] Yesterday Hunterbrook Media also published a related, shorter story a=
bout Danieli & C. S.p.A., a publicly traded Italian company, which allegedl=
y =E2=80=9Chas been involved in restarting junta-owned steel mills in Myanm=
ar with links to arms manufacturers=E2=80=9D; there too Hunterbrook Capital=
 took no positions.


  [5] The really daring one is the stock borrow requirement: If the governm=
ent just allowed naked short selling, it would be cheaper and easier to sho=
rt stocks! But naked short selling makes people much, much, much angrier th=
an regular short selling, so I can only mention this one in a footnote.

></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When Trump Media went public as a meme s=
tock, it=C2=A0set off a race between its business and its big shareholders=
=E2=80=99 ability to sell. This week brought news on both sides =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0the streaming plans for the business, the warrant share registration =
for the sales =E2=80=94=C2=A0and, uh. The stock is down.</p><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">Meanwhile here is a quite grim <a href=3D"https://link.mail.b=
loombergbusiness.com/click/35055872.287342/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud2FzaGluZ3RvbnBv=

on: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000=
; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Washington Post stor=
y</a> interviewing some of the shareholders:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">Jerry Dean McLain first bet on former president Donald Trump'=
s Truth Social two years ago, buying into the Trump company's planned merge=
r partner, Digital World Acquisition, at $90 a share. Over time, as the pri=
ce changed, he kept buying, amassing hundreds of shares for $25,000 =E2=80=
=94 pretty much his "whole nest egg," he said.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px =
0;">That nest egg has lost about half its value in the past two weeks as Tr=
ump Media & Technology Group's share price dropped from $66 after its p=
ublic debut last month to $32 on Friday. But McLain, 71, who owns a tree-re=
moval service outside Oklahoma City, said he's not worried. If anything, he=
 wants to buy more.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">"I know good and well i=
t's in Trump's hands, and he's got plans," he said. "I have no doubt it's g=
oing to explode sometime."=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=
;">As with many meme stocks, there are conspiracy theories about what could=
 possibly be keeping Trump Media trading at only 800 times trailing revenue=
s:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Todd Schlanger, an interior =

have been the result of "stock manipulation" from an "organized effort" to =
make the company look bad. There's no proof of such a campaign, but Schlang=
er is convinced. "It's got to be political," he said, from all the "liberal=
s that are trying to knock it down." ...</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Ca=
rol Swain, a prominent conservative commentator in Nashville who previously=

stock manipulation, arguing that "the people who hate Donald Trump would do=
 anything to try to hurt him." As for Truth Social itself, she said she pos=
ts there only sparingly and prefers X, where she has 35 times as many follo=
wers. "I have always wanted not to just preach to the choir," she said.</p>=

e they're working on something amazing and new.</p></blockquote><p style=3D=

ockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But right at the top there is a state=

lure by subsidizing activist short selling. If you are an activist short se=

ou turn out to be right, the government should write you a check =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0in=C2=A0<em>addition=C2=A0</em>to your winnings on your trade =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0to encourage you.</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When I wri=
te it like that it seems =E2=80=A6=C2=A0wrong? As a general matter, it=E2=
=80=99s not obvious that the best use of government resources is to subsidi=
ze=C2=A0<em>any=C2=A0</em>sort of hedge funds. Also, some of the problems w=
ith short selling are imposed by the government =E2=80=94=C2=A0the hazy cri=
minal investigations, for instance<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#foot=
note-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-b=
ottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !=
important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;=
">
<span>[5]</span>
</a>=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0and it might be more efficient to get rid of those=
 problems than to offset them with subsidies.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=
;">Nonetheless this is a real thing that the government actually does! Sort=
 of. Here=E2=80=99s a fun paper by Alexander Platt of the University of Kan=
sas School of Law titled =E2=80=9C<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbus=
iness.com/click/35055872.287342/aHR0cHM6Ly9wYXBlcnMuc3Nybi5jb20vc29sMy9wYXB=

mprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #c=
cc;">The Shortseller Enrichment Commission?: Whistleblowers, Activist Short=
 Sellers, and the New Privatization of Public Enforcement</a>=E2=80=9D:</p>=
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Two developments have transformed =
the detection of corporate fraud in the last decade: the SEC whistleblower =
bounty program (WBP) and the rise of activist short sellers. The WBP offers=
 up financial bounties to individuals who bring forward actionable informat=
ion about securities fraud. Activist shorts conduct due diligence to identi=
fy overvalued public companies, take short positions, reveal the negative i=
nformation, and then enjoy trading profits if and when the stock tanks. Con=
sidered separately, these institutions are widely regarded as socially valu=
able innovations that help deter fraud.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But=
, it turns out, they are not fully separate. Activist shorts have been part=
icipating actively and effectively in the WBP =E2=80=93 both directly (subm=
itting tips, filing claims, and winning awards) and indirectly (partnering =
with insider tipsters). Their participation has transformed the WBP into an=
 undercover outsourcing program; a new way for the SEC to pay private profe=
ssionals to do work that traditionally has been done by SEC staff.</p><p st=
yle=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This privatization might be defensible, indeed laud=
able, if it yielded more efficient deterrence than what the SEC could achie=
ve on its own. Unfortunately, it likely does not. The SEC may be paying act=
ivist shorts for information they would have made public even without the p=
rospect of bounty (because of their trading strategy). In such cases, the p=
ublic bounty payment does nothing to incentivize additional fraud detection=
 and is merely a windfall for the recipient. Those funds could be better sp=
ent by expanding the SEC=E2=80=99s own enforcement capacity.</p></blockquot=
e><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombe=
rgbusiness.com/click/35055872.287342/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcG=
luaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMTEtMTYvaGFja2Vycy1rbm93LWV2ZXJ5dGhpbmctaXMtc2Vjd=

owing.=C2=A0Notably, when we <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness=

published investigation of a public company, I noted that it shorted the st=

5px;">Silence is not securities fraud</h2>

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Under US securities law, if a company=
=C2=A0<em>says something false</em>, it can be sued for securities fraud, b=
ut if it=C2=A0<em>neglects to mention something true and important,=C2=A0</=
em>it can=E2=80=99t. There are lots of qualifications to that second part, =
to the point that I kind of discount it, but we talked yesterday about a Su=
preme Court decision holding that =E2=80=9Cpure omissions=E2=80=9D are not =
securities fraud. <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/=
35055872.287342/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzI=

le=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width=
: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background=
-color: #ccc;">I wrote</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You=
 could imagine pure silence being misleading. For instance, you could imagi=
ne things that the market would=C2=A0<em>expect to be disclosed=C2=A0</em>i=
f they happened, so that=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>disclosing them sort of imp=
lies that they didn=E2=80=99t happen. If a company=E2=80=99s chief executiv=
e officer died suddenly, and the company didn=E2=80=99t tell anyone for a f=
ew weeks, that would be weird! The company hasn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0<em>lied</em=
>, but everyone kind of went around assuming that the CEO was alive, and th=
e company didn=E2=80=99t bother to correct their mistaken impression.</p></=
blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Readers emailed to suggest consider=

!important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-=
bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">2023 paper that he wrote</a> =
with Seungjoon Oh with this amazing footnote:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"ma=
rgin: 16px 0;">As an extreme example, one of the sample firms in this study=
, Badger Paper Mills, delayed its announcement of the death of its CEO for =
about two months until the next scheduled regular board meeting. According =
to one of the directors, =E2=80=9Cthe board decided to wait to disclose the=
 search for Cosgrove=E2=80=99s replacement until it also had news of a new =
board member=E2=80=9D (Milwaukee Journal Sentinel, October 30, 2000). Inter=
estingly, the SEC explicitly states that the death of an officer or directo=
r does not trigger an 8-K filing (https://www.sec.gov/divisions/corpfin/gui=
dance/8-kinterp.htm).</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And Evan=
 Hughes=C2=A0pointed out a slightly different real case=C2=A0<a href=3D"htt=
ps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35055872.287342/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cu=

: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background=
-color: #ccc;">from Sweden=C2=A0in 2016</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">Swedish measurement technology firm Hexagon has defended the =
time it took to announce the arrest of its chief executive for alleged insi=
der trading after it came to light he was under arrest during last week's e=
arnings call with analysts.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">After being det=
ained in Sweden on Oct. 26, Ola Rollen was allowed by the Swedish Economic =
Crime Authority to present Hexagon's third-quarter results in a conference =
call on Oct. 28, the agency told Reuters on Tuesday, adding two of its poli=
ce officers were with Rollen during the call.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=
;">Analysts on the call were not told Rollen had been arrested or that poli=
ce were in the room.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yes=
